skill.name: code-patterns
skill.description: Reference guide for common TypeScript design, async, error handling, and data access patterns.
skill.summary: Quick TypeScript pattern reference for choosing and adapting common implementation approaches.
skill.author: Benny9193

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown fences, template literals, and words such as description as risky code patterns. Manual review found only documentation and TypeScript examples, with no executable shell commands, weak cryptography, database files, reconnaissance behavior, network access, or prompt injection text.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings are Markdown code fences or TypeScript template literals inside examples. No Ruby, shell, or command execution behavior is present in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are Markdown fence delimiters or TypeScript string interpolation examples. The file is documentation and contains no executable Ruby or shell command invocation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Terms Are Plain Text
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings appear to match substrings in descriptive text and method names such as description. No cryptographic algorithm, hash function, encryption call, or security protocol is shown.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual line review confirms these references are metadata or TypeScript interface methods. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or another weak cryptographic implementation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Database References Are Example Code
low_findings.description: The sensitive database findings are TypeScript data access examples using a parameterized query and transaction method names. No SQLite database file, embedded data, or credential material is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are illustrative TypeScript repository and unit-of-work snippets. They do not contain an actual database file path or database contents.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Indicators Are Example Text
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance findings occur in prose, interfaces, template literals, type parameters, or a sample SQL query. No operating system, host, process, network, or environment discovery command is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line context contains no reconnaissance commands or APIs. The matches are ordinary documentation and TypeScript syntax.

content.value_statement: Developers often need a fast way to choose a suitable implementation pattern. This skill provides concise TypeScript examples for design patterns, async control, error handling, and data access.
actual_capabilities: Provides TypeScript examples for factory, builder, singleton, adapter, decorator, strategy, and observer patterns.
Explains when to use each listed pattern in short practical notes.
Shows error handling examples for result types and retry with backoff.
Includes async control examples for promise queues and debouncing.
Covers data access examples for repository and unit of work approaches.
limitations: The examples are starting points and may need adaptation for production systems.
It does not inspect a project or automatically refactor code.
It focuses on TypeScript-style examples rather than every programming language.
Some snippets omit surrounding types or infrastructure for brevity.

use_cases.title: Choose an Implementation Pattern
use_cases.description: Compare common patterns before adding a new feature or service boundary.

use_cases.title: Review Architecture Options
use_cases.description: Use the pattern table to discuss tradeoffs during code review or design planning.

use_cases.title: Learn TypeScript Pattern Basics
use_cases.description: Study compact examples that show each pattern's purpose and basic structure.

prompt_templates.title: Find a Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to solve this problem: [describe problem]. Which pattern from the code-patterns skill fits best, and why?

prompt_templates.title: Adapt an Example
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the code-patterns skill to adapt the [pattern name] example for this TypeScript use case: [describe use case].

prompt_templates.title: Compare Alternatives
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare [pattern A] and [pattern B] for this feature: [describe feature]. Include tradeoffs and a recommendation.

prompt_templates.title: Review Existing Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this design against the patterns in the code-patterns skill: [describe design]. Identify mismatches, risks, and cleaner alternatives.

output_examples.input: Which pattern should I use for interchangeable payment methods?
output_examples.output: Strategy is the best fit because the payment algorithm changes at runtime while the checkout flow stays stable.

output_examples.input: How should I coordinate several repository writes?
output_examples.output: Unit of Work is appropriate when multiple operations must commit or roll back together as one transaction.

output_examples.input: What pattern helps with expected validation failures?
output_examples.output: A Result type can make expected failures explicit without using exceptions for normal control flow.

best_practices: Start from the problem statement before selecting a pattern.
Adapt examples to the project's naming, error handling, and dependency style.
Keep pattern use small until repeated complexity justifies the structure.
anti_patterns: Do not add a pattern only because it is familiar.
Do not use singleton state where dependency injection would be clearer.
Do not copy snippets without adding production error handling and tests.

faq.question: Does this skill generate complete production modules?
faq.answer: No. It provides concise reference patterns that should be adapted and tested for each project.

faq.question: Is this skill specific to TypeScript?
faq.answer: The examples use TypeScript syntax, but many ideas apply to other object-oriented or typed languages.

faq.question: Can this skill choose a pattern for my feature?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide the feature constraints, and the assistant can map them to relevant patterns.

faq.question: Does it include security guidance?
faq.answer: Only indirectly. It is a code pattern reference, not a secure coding checklist.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Should I copy the examples exactly?
faq.answer: No. Treat them as starting points and adjust them for project standards, tests, and runtime requirements.
